description:
You are acting as a cloud-infrastructure architect who is about to migrate several legacy
micro-services to a new region.  
Before touching anything in the cloud you must create a clean, verifiable local backup of
the current configuration directory that lives at:

    /home/user/source_configs/

That directory already exists and currently contains exactly three files.  
Nothing in that directory should be modified; your job is only to back it up and
produce evidence that the backup is complete and un-corrupted.

1. Create a sub-directory called

    /home/user/backups/

   if it does not yet exist.  
   All other artefacts mentioned below must be written inside that directory.

2. Pack *only* the contents of /home/user/source_configs/ into a compressed **gzip
   tar-archive** named

    /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.tar.gz

   The archive must NOT include an extra top-level path segment; when one lists the
   archive, it should show the three files as plain relative paths:
        config1.yaml
        config2.ini
        app.env

3. Produce a SHA-256 checksum file named

    /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.sha256

   It must contain exactly one line, in the canonical output format of the
   `sha256sum` utility:

        <64-character-hex-digest><two-spaces>/home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.tar.gz

   (That is: digest, *two* spaces, absolute path to the tarball, and a trailing
   newline.)

4. Generate a human-readable manifest file named

    /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.manifest

   The manifest must contain the list of files **inside** the archive, one per line,
   sorted alphabetically and with no leading ‘‘./’’ components, e.g.

        app.env
        config1.yaml
        config2.ini

   A trailing newline after the last line **is required**.

5. Verification hint (what the automated grader will check):
   • The directory /home/user/backups/ exists.  
   • The tarball exists and is a valid gzip archive.  
   • `tar -tzf /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.tar.gz` prints exactly the
     three file names above, nothing else, and in any order.  
   • The manifest file exists, contains exactly three non-empty lines, sorted
     alphabetically, matching what `tar -tzf` returns.  
   • The checksum file exists, has exactly one line, the second field matches the
     absolute path of the tarball, and the first field is a 64-char lowercase
     hexadecimal string.

Use any legitimate shell commands you like, but keep the overall process to roughly
three or four commands.  When you are finished, the backup directory should contain
exactly three new files:

    source_configs_backup.tar.gz
    source_configs_backup.sha256
    source_configs_backup.manifest
truth:
Initial filesystem state (already present before the agent starts):

1. Directory:
/home/user/source_configs/

2. Files and exact contents (with Unix LF line-endings):

/home/user/source_configs/config1.yaml
--------------------------------------
---
service: web
replicas: 3
<newline>

/home/user/source_configs/config2.ini
-------------------------------------
[database]
host = db.internal
port = 5432
<newline>

/home/user/source_configs/app.env
---------------------------------
APP_ENV=production
DEBUG=false
<newline>

The `/home/user/backups/` directory does NOT exist initially.

Expected artefacts after successful task completion:

A. Directory:
/home/user/backups/

B. Files:

B1. /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.tar.gz
    • Valid gzip-compressed tar archive.
    • When listed via `tar -tzf`, it produces exactly:
          app.env
          config1.yaml
          config2.ini

B2. /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.sha256
    • Exactly one line.
    • Regex: ^[a-f0-9]{64}  /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup\.tar\.gz\n$

B3. /home/user/backups/source_configs_backup.manifest
    • Exactly three lines, each terminated by `\n`, in this order:
          app.env\n
          config1.yaml\n
          config2.ini\n
name: task_000000_2f7fdae4
